text: The surgical setup includes a 12mm right-hand working port for stapler introduction, with an optional epigastric port for stomach retraction visible in the laparoscopic view.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac, focusing on the left side where the layers are not fused, avoiding the mesocolon on the right side.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac using a harmonic device, carefully preserving the gastrovibrioic arcade. The short gastric vessels are divided, with particular attention to the uppermost short gastric to prevent avulsion and bleeding. The posterior gastric artery, branching from the splenic artery and entering the stomach, is also divided to separate the stomach from the upper pole of the spleen.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac to the right side, revealing approximately three to four centimeters of the pancreas beyond the tumor, preparing for further dissection.

The surgeon uses a stitch to hitch the stomach onto the anterior abdominal wall, providing effective retraction and keeping the stomach clear of the surgical field during the splenectomy and distal pancreatectomy.

The surgeon begins the pancreatic dissection by locating the splenic artery at the upper border of the pancreas. The peritoneum is opened, and surrounding fat is dissected away to reveal the artery, identified by its pulsations. The artery is controlled and slung, with careful attention to avoid injuring the vasa-vasorum to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon applies gentle traction to dissect a substantial length of the splenic artery, which supplies the distal pancreas and spleen. The artery is then controlled using styes or hemologs to prevent bleeding and congestion during subsequent dissection.

During the dissection of loose areolar tissue, the surgeon identifies a polar branch to the spleen arising from the proximal pancreatic stump and a small pancreatic branch from the splenic artery entering the specimen. These arteries are carefully identified and divided.

The surgeon divides the remaining peritoneum at the upper border of the pancreas, carefully avoiding injury to the left gastric vessels. The splenic flexure of the colon is then mobilized to improve access to the distal body and tail of the pancreas. The peritoneum on the lower border of the pancreas is opened, providing access to the retropancreatic planes.

The surgeon develops the retropancreatic plane, an avascular area containing loose areolar tissue, using blunt dissection and occasional harmonic device application. The pancreas and splenic vein are lifted anteriorly, while posteriorly, the kidney, renal vein, and aorta are visible, bound by the geratous fascia and its medial extensions.

The surgeon carefully dissects the splenic vein, which appears engorged and thin-walled due to the presence of a large tumor and spleen. The dissection is performed gently to prevent injury and bleeding, while facilitating further retro pancreatic dissection to increase the working space.

The surgeon ensures an adequate retro-pancreatic space is created and dissects a sufficient length of the splenic vein. The splenic vein is then slung, clipped, and divided, with ligation performed using ties or hemologs.
The surgeon separates the pancreas from the genital fascia by dissecting the loose areolar tissue laterally, progressing towards the lino renal ligament after ligating the splenic artery and vein.

The surgeon performs pancreatic transduction by creating a plane across the full thickness of the pancreas, starting from the lower border and extending to the upper border, ensuring the exclusion of vessels. The pancreas remains fixed and stable at both ends before dividing the linoleum ligament.

An umbilical tape is placed across the retropancreatic plane, and lifting it aids in further dissection and widening of the plane. The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to create a window large enough for the introduction of the Endo GIA stapler, ensuring that no proximal vascular stumps, particularly arterial stumps, are in the staple line.

The surgeon identifies the point of parenchymal transection in the pancreas and creates an adequate retroperitoneal tunnel for the smooth introduction of the endo-GIA stapler. A small feeder branch from the left colic vessels is divided. The lower part of the pancreas and tumor are separated from the mesocolon until reaching the avascular lino renal ligament, which is lifted and divided quickly.
The surgeon leaves some fat attached to the spleen to facilitate lifting and holding it during transection, preventing splenic tears and maintaining a clear surgical field.
The surgeon applies upward traction on the pancreatic tissue, lifting the pancreas-spleen complex and facilitating the division of the lino renal ligament.
The surgeon develops a white pancreatic tunnel using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, preparing the area for the introduction of the endo-GIA stapler.

The surgeon frees the upper border of the tumor and the upper pole of the spleen from the retroperitoneum and the left crus, completing the mobilization. The pancreatic transection is prepared, with the option to use a stapler for thin pancreas or hand-sewn technique for thick pancreas, either laparoscopically or through a small midline incision.

The surgeon uses an Endo-GIA stapler with a gold reload due to the thickness of the pancreas. The stapler is applied with adequate compression for about three minutes to ensure effective hemostasis before firing.

The surgeon clips and divides a small sliver of pancreatic tissue that was not transected by the stapler. Following this, the remaining retroperitoneal tissue tethering the tumor to the retroperitoneum is carefully divided to complete the resection.

The surgeon pushes the resected specimen down into the pelvis for retrieval through a small incision using a specimen bag. Retroperitoneal lavage is performed, and hemostasis is secured. The anesthetist increases blood pressure to check the vascular stumps and staple line for any bleeding.

The surgeon applies Nectacryl glue to the pancreatic staple line to enhance hemostasis. A surgical drain is placed in the left upper quadrant. The resected pancreatic tissue is identified as a solid papilloneoplasm upon postoperative histological examination.
